measured.transcript: We will hear argument this morning in case twenty two seventy-four Glossop versus Oklahoma. Mr. Waxman. Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the court, Richard Glossop was convicted on the word of one man, Justin Sneed, the undisputed murderer in this case. Oklahoma has now disclosed evidence revealing that Mr. Sneed lied to the jury about his history of psychiatric treatment, including the fact that a prison psychiatrist prescribed lithium to treat his previously undiagnosed bipolar disorder. The prosecution suppressed that evidence and then failed to correct Mr. Sneed's perjured denial, just as it suppressed evidence that in the middle of trial, in violation of the court's sequestration order, Sneed altered his testimony about the knife wounds the victim at the urgent request of the prosecutor, who then denied to the court her prior knowledge. There is no adequate or independent jurisdictional bar to review and no warrant for an evidentiary hearing. As to independence, the court's opinion is suffused with merit's determination on the Brady and Napo claims, and certainly there is no, quote, clear and express statement that the Court's decision is based on a bona fidee, separate, adequate, and independent grounds as long required by this court to preclude review, nor is there any adequate bar. By rejecting the state's waiver, the court created a jurisdictional threshold it had never applied in any other case. The disposition was, quote, without support in prior state law, as required by over 60 years of this court's precedent to establish adequacy. No evidentiary hearing could alter the conclusion that Mr. Glossop was denied due process. There's no dispute that, contrary to Sneed's sworn testimony, the state's own suppressed record shows he was in fact treated by a psychiatrist for bipolar disorder, just as there is no dispute that Snead changed his testimony the knife at the urgent trial request of the prosecutor, who then falsely denied that very fact to the court. This court should reverse and remand for a new trial. I welcome the court's questions. Mr. Waxman, you place quite a bit of weight on the note from Smotherman and Ackley and from your opening statement, you clearly do not agree with them. Did you at any point get a statement from either one of the prosecutors? Yes. Did you interview them? Well, to be clear, we we did get a sworn statement, which I believe at page 960 of the joint appendix from Gary Ackley in which for among other things he never mentions the fact that he never mentions the account that he is now provided to the in the amicus brief for the Van Treese family. And as to Ms. Smotherman, Ms. Smotherman was interviewed both by the independent the legislature's independent counsel and by the Attorney independent counsel, Mr. Duncan. She gave different answers each none of which was the account she's now provided in an letter attached to the Van Treese brief. Mm-hmm. Well it would seem that, because not only their reputations are being impugned, but they are central to this case, it would seem that they the an interview of these two prosecutors would be central. They suggest that the their interviews were generally about prosecuting capital cases and not specifically about the details of this. Look, their current unsworn statement, appended at the very last minute for the very first time in a merits amicus brief before this court, deserves all the benefit of the doubt that they you know to which they are entitled. In the context of Mr. Ackley did file an affidavit, it is in the record in this case, and it is not in any way consistent his current account. Did they make themselves unavailable? Sorry to interrupt. Well, he made himself available. He provided us a declaration. No, it is uh what you are saying is would make sense if for some reason they had made themselves unavailable. They suggest that they were not sought out and given an opportunity to do that.
